entry.title: Joule Work & Knowledge Graphs - From Thought to Execution in Seconds - No Clicks. Just Results.
entry.content:
What if enterprise systems could execute what you have in mind—your goal, your need—without requiring you to navigate applications, workflows, or data manually?

That’s exactly what we’ll explore in this special live session on July 8 (15:00 CEST / 09:00 New York / 18:30 Bengaluru).

 Why This Matters

Today’s enterprises are full of complexity—fragmented systems, disconnected data, and manual steps.

This session shows how to:

  • Move from describing a need to seeing it executed
  • Let AI orchestrate processes across systems automatically
  • Use the Knowledge Graph to ensure every action is grounded in real business context
  • Deliver systems that feel simpler, faster, and more aligned with how people think

Who Should Join

This is relevant for anyone shaping or using enterprise systems:

  • Developers
  • Architects
  • Data & AI professionals
  • Product managers
  • Business analysts
  • Transformation & innovation leaders
  • IT decision-makers

What You’ll Learn

  • How Joule Work translates goals into actions
  • How Knowledge Graphs bring context and meaning to AI
  • What needs to be designed:
    • APIs
    • Event-driven architectures
    • Semantic models

Join Us Live

📅July 8
⏰15:00 CEST / 09:00 EDT/ 18:30 IST

💬 Why join live?
Interact directly with the expert, ask questions, and get instant insights.

⏯️ Can’t make it?

entry.title: Re: Manage Fixed Assets – Main Number of Original Asset -...
entry.content:
Dear @Nozomu_Kihara,

The Legacy Main Number - Subnumber field is not intended as a manually maintained business reference field in the Manage Fixed Assets app. Based on standard behavior, it can be populated during fixed asset migration / legacy asset transfer, but there is no standard option in the Manage Fixed Assets app to enter or maintain this field directly for a newly created regular fixed asset. The reason is that this field is a one-time technical reference populated in the legacy asset transfer / fixed asset migration context, used as part of the legacy data transfer process to retain the reference to the asset number from the source system. It is not part of the standard maintainable screen layout in the Manage Fixed Assets app, and SAP does not provide a supported way to expose this specific field for manual maintenance

More details below:

SAP Help – Fixed asset - Master data
SAP Help – Transfer Legacy Assets with the SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit

For a standard field alternative the best fit is usually Inventory Number. It is a standard fixed asset master data field intended for asset identification/tracking purposes and it can be maintained as part of the asset master data. Therefore it is the recommended standard field to store the fixed asset management number from the external system, provided it is not already used for another internal inventory/tagging purpose

More details below:

SAP Help – Manage Fixed Assets
SAP Learning – Maintaining Asset Master Data

The recommended approach is:

  • Do not use Legacy Main Number - Subnumber for ongoing manual maintenance in Manage Fixed Assets
  • Use Inventory Number to store the fixed asset management number from the external system, if this field is available for the customer’s business use
  • If Inventory Number is also reserved or does not meet the required format/length/reporting need then the clean standard extensibility option is to create a dedicated custom field for the external fixed asset reference and expose it in the Manage Fixed Assets app. SAP provides extensibility for fixed asset master data in the Manage Fixed Assets app

More details below:

SAP Help – App Extensibility: Manage Fixed Assets

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Advanced intercompany STO Sales Area Mismatch in Erro...
entry.content:
This error usually happens because the system is trying to validate the ship‑to party against a different sales area than the one you configured. In your case, although the STO is set up with 32CN / C1 / D0, the error message is referencing 32CN / C1 / 00 — which suggests a mismatch in partner determination or customer master extension. The system is deriving the “00” division from default settings or missing master data.

Having said that, the system is not “inventing” 32CN / C1 / 00; it is defaulting to it because the ship‑to party master data is missing the correct division (D0). Extending the business partner to the right sales area (32CN / C1 / D0) or correcting the STO customizing should resolve the error.

entry.title: Re: What is important (doc. number) in the layout to jump...
entry.content:
Why the jump differs is because

  • In layout /TCO_01, the field used for drill‑down is the original FI document number (BKPF/Accounting document). That’s why clicking takes you to the invoice document.
  • In layout /DSW, the field used is the CO document number (COEP/Controlling document). So the system navigates to the controlling document instead.
  • Even though both layouts display a “Reference document number,” the technical field assignment behind that column differs. SAP Note 2445603 explains that the error KB420 occurs when the wrong field is used for navigation.

If you want consistent behavior, ensure your layout uses the FI document number (BELNR) rather than the CO document number.

entry.title: Re: France E-Invoices and E-Reports: The Cloud Edition as Your Approved Platform
entry.content:
Hi @petranagy-baranyi87,

Thank you for your question. Let’s me reply in parts:

Yes, France requires two service bindings.  In the SAP Help Portal documentation, when integrating with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you can check the step Creating Service Bindings to find detailed information about the additional service binding for France. But, at the end, you will have one binding of type Peppol Exchange, and one of type Other Processes. You do all subsequent configuration using the service binding of type Other Processes. 

However, before you go further, I suggest you check which BTP region your subaccount is in. I am not sure what you mean by "I have the correct version or DRC", but kind reminder that the E-Invoices and E-Reports (France) process is only available in the eu02 region. For more information on the regions, see Subaccount Regions for Processes in the Cloud Edition documentation.

If your current subaccount is not eu02, you will need to create a separate subaccount specifically for France. You just need to be careful to create a country-specific communication system and communication arrangement in the new subaccount. For more information, see Creating Communication Systems.

Hope this helps you.

Best regards, Aleshane

entry.title: Re: Requirement to Carry Forward G/L Balances During Vend...
entry.content:
Hi S0027020516,

Short answer: No — there is no standard function that "carries G/L balances into vendor accounts" as part of the supplier upload. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the Supplier object loads master data only; vendor balances are migrated separately as AP open items, and those postings update the assigned reconciliation G/L account automatically.

Clarifying the requirement (my interpretation — please confirm)

The wording mixes two different objects. A vendor account is an AP subledger account — it has no independent "G/L balance." Its balance is the sum of its open items, which roll up automatically into the reconciliation account defined in the business partner (supplier role). All postings to the subledger accounts are automatically posted to the assigned reconciliation accounts (or if defined and allowed, to the alternative reconciliation account), so the flow is the reverse of what the requirement describes: subledger postings feed the G/L, not the other way around.

Is balance take-on supported inside the supplier upload? — No

No. The Supplier migration object is master data only. Migration objects are built for initial migration of your data... you can create data with a migration object, but you can't change or update existing data with it — and no master-data object carries monetary balances. SAP Help Portal

Recommended process, app, and migration objects — via the standard cockpit

Use the Migrate Your Data app (SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit); it is an embedded tool included at no additional cost. Standard finance go-live sequence: SAP Learning

  • Supplier — master data; migrated first (predecessor for the open items).
  • FI - Accounts payable open item — the vendor balances. The following posting is made during migration of vendor open items: PK 31 – Vendor - Ct 100, PK 40 – Migration Account - Dt 100 — this automatically builds the AP reconciliation account balance. Reference: SAP Help and SAP Community
  • FI - G/L account balance and open/line item — opening balances for the remaining (non-subledger) G/L accounts only.
  • FI - Historical Balance (optional) — prior-year balances for reporting; only the ACDOCA table (Universal Journal) will undergo migration... only balances are migrated, and no documents are generated, and they auto-reverse on the key date. Reference: KBA 2943035 and SAP Community

Important:

  • Do not double-load the reconciliation account. It gets its balance from FI - Accounts payable open item. Do not also load it via the G/L balance object, or payables will be doubled — the G/L balance object must exclude AR/AP reconciliation accounts.

Constraints to verify:

  • A migration/data take-on clearing G/L account must be configured; the net of all take-on postings must be zero after cutover.
  • Special G/L items (bills of exchange, noted items) may not load through the standard AP open item object — verify for your release. Reference: KBA 2934405
  • Visible migration objects depend on your activated scope and chosen migration approach (Staging Table vs. Direct Transfer).

Summary

There is no "G/L-to-vendor balance carry-forward" step. Migrate Supplier (master data) first, then FI - Accounts payable open item for the vendor balances — the reconciliation account updates itself — and use FI - G/L account balance and open/line item for all other G/L accounts, keeping the reconciliation accounts out of it to avoid double counting.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Unable to scrap Assets 40580 & 40584 Error During Asset Retirement.
entry.content:
We are unable to retire the following assets in production:
Company Code: DDN, Assets: 40580 & 40584
Earlier we faced a fiscal year closing issue, which has now been resolved successfully.
However, while performing Asset Retirement by Scrapping(ABAVN), THE SYSTEM ISSUES THE FOLLOWING ERROR: "Message AA465- Posting of retirement not possible(error in the asset)".
We alsoexecuted AFAR, checked the depreciation key(YLIN), depreciation area 06, and Asset Explorer(AW01N).
SAP previously confirmed that depreciation key YLIN has a 5% scrap value (IND), and both assets have already reached net book value equals to zero, indicating that the issue is related to system configuration rather than an SAP software defect.
We request guidance on the correct configuration or business process required to successfully retire/scrap these two assets without causing inconsistency.

entry.title: Re: Requirement to Carry Forward G/L Balances During Vend...
entry.content:
Dear @S0027020516,

This is not supported as part of the Supplier/Vendor master data upload in Public Cloud. The Supplier migration object is used for supplier master data migration. It does not provide a function to carry forward financial balances or automatically assign existing G/L balances to individual supplier accounts during the vendor upload process. Supplier/AP open balances are handled by a separate transactional migration object

Recommended approach:

  • First migrate the supplier master data using the Supplier migration object in the Migrate Your Data (F3473) app
  • Then migrate the supplier open balances separately using FI - Accounts payable open item. SAP describes this object as the object used to load Accounts Payable open items also known as supplier open items

  • Do not migrate the same AP amount again as a G/L balance to the supplier reconciliation account. AP open items should be migrated through the AP open item migration object, not by direct posting to the supplier reconciliation account
  • After completing the migration, verify the initial offset accounts. If balances remain on the offset accounts the standard follow-up is to check that the combined total nets to zero and then clear the offset accounts with a manual journal entry. This is a post-migration cleanup activity, not an automatic transfer during supplier upload

More details below:

SAP Help – Supplier
SAP Help – FI - Accounts payable open item
SAP Help – Available Migration Objects
SAP KBA 2853964 – Migration Cockpit: Collective KBA for G/L Open Item or G/L Balance or FI AP Open Item or FI AR Open Item or FI - Historical balance
SAP KBA 3757771 – Post-Migration Clearing of Initial Offset Accounts in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: 🇳🇱 AI-enabled integrations with SAP Integration Suite (Utrecht, Netherlands)
entry.content:
In this SAP CodeJam, we will look at SAP Integration Suite and different AI features that we can leverage to integrate our systems and help improve our integration development activities. We will receive customer request events from a customer website, process them in SAP Cloud Integration with the help of an LLM hosted in SAP AI Core. Then, that information will be sent to the Customer service request system. Once we complete the integration flow we will get familiar with the new MCP Gateway available in API Management. We will expose an existing MCP server via the Gateway and we will create an MCP server for an existing API. To wrap things up, we will configure our new MCP server as tools available to an LLM and interact with them from a chat interface. By the end of the CodeJam, we will achieve a scenario like the one below in the diagram.

By the end of the CodeJam, we will achieve a scenario like the one below in the diagram.

 

Prerequisites

  • Bring your own laptop with power adapter and have a supported Web browser such as Google Chrome or Firefox installed on your laptop.
  • It can be helpful to bring a tablet or second laptop, if you have, to have the instructions on a separate screen.

Location and Date 

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is planned for Friday, September 18, 2026 from 13:00 - 19:00 local time in Utrecht, Netherlands (CEST). The language of the event and the content will be English.

Event Address: INNOV8iON, Boteyken 323, 3454 PD Utrecht, The Netherlands

  • The event is kindly being hosted by INNOV8iON – THANK YOU!
  • The instructor is SAP Developer Advocate Antonio Maradiaga

Please note that spaces are limited! We can only accommodate 30 attendees. If you want to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right. About 2 weeks before the event, you will be asked to confirm your attendance and will receive further instructions.

entry.title: Download DMEEX Parent- Child tree
entry.content:
Hi Team,

As part of our transition to S/4HANA, we have set up a parent–child structure for DMEE. The parent DMEE contains multiple child DMEEs to meet country‑specific requirements.

In ECC, as a precaution, we used to download the XML of the DMEE structure before making any changes, so that we always had a backup in case something went wrong.

In the S/4 landscape, our parent DMEE is ZXX_XML_CT_M, and the child DMEEs are ZXX_XML_CT_AE, ZXX_XML_CT_US, ZXX_XML_CT_CA, and others.

My question is: How can we download or back up the complete parent–child DMEE structure?

When we attempt to download, the system only downloads ZEY_XML_CT_M. Does this mean we must download the parent DMEE separately and then download each child DMEE individually? If so, it also implies that restoring the structure would require multiple uploads (parent, child1, child2, etc.) rather than a single DMEE import.

Please advise if there is a way to export the entire parent–child DMEE hierarchy in one go ?

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Planning Now Available in Support Content Repository
entry.content:
What's New?

We're excited to announce a new dedicated topic SAP Analytics Cloud Planning in our Support Content repository! This addition brings together essential guidance on planning capabilities to help you make the most of your strategic initiatives.

What You'll Find There

When end users encounter issues with SAP Analytics Cloud Planning, for example a cell that won't accept input, a data action that writes nothing, they need guidance and answers that are not covered or beyond the standard Online Help and existing KBAs. That is exactly why we published this Support Content: written to bridge the gap between "something is wrong" and "here is exactly why, and here is what to do about it."

It captures the detailed configuration steps with screenshots, nuanced product behaviors, known limitations, and non-obvious root causes with samples, so that audience can understand, resolve issues faster and gain knowledge from the documentation.

The current SAC Planning Support Content covers the following topics:

  • Data Entry & Publishing - How the two-phase workflow protects data integrity, and what to check when users see inconsistent data between sessions.
  • Planning Area - Why configuring this feature in Model Settings can transform performance on large models, and the edge cases that affect cell inputability.
  • Data Locking & Data Access Control - How two independent gatekeepers interact, and the most common reasons edits are silently blocked.
  • Validation Rules & Member Derivation - Enforcing valid dimension combinations at the model level, including disaggregation behavior and current limits.
  • Data Actions - Queuing mechanics, the CONFIG.HIERARCHY setting that fixes the most common "zero records affected" mystery, and when to use synchronous mode.
  • Audit Log Management - Row limits, cleanup cadence recommendations, and version size impact.
  • Scripting Best Practices - API pitfalls around getMembers(), filter format mismatches, and the scripting patterns that cause render loops or unnecessary backend calls.

Whether you are an implementation consultant, customer administrator, or support engineer, this Support Content provides clear, actionable guidance to turn confusing problems into resolved ones, and it continues to evolve with new and refined topics.

Share Your Feedback

This is a growing resource! So if you have any questions about the content, please go ahead with the embedded feedback or comment feature.

Learn More About Support Content

The Support Content repository is the place to find content created and maintained by SAP Support engineers experties on their areas. Explore this repository with additional documentation provided to help the SAP ecosystem.

entry.title: SF Skills Proficiency: What Business Value Are You Seeing from Multi-Level Proficiency Scales?
entry.content:
Hello SuccessFactors customer colleagues,

We are currently reviewing our skills strategy and would love to learn from organizations that have practical experience with Skills Proficiency in SuccessFactors.

Many discussions focus on how to configure proficiency scales, but we are particularly interested in understanding the actual business value, adoption, and outcomes organizations are seeing.

Our Questions

1. What proficiency scale are you using today?

Examples:

  • Binary only (Have Skill / Don't Have Skill)
  • 3-point scale
  • 5-point scale
  • 7-point or higher scale
  • Different scales for different skill catalogs
  • No proficiency ratings at all

2. Why did you choose that approach?

Were your decisions driven by:

  • Simplicity and employee adoption?
  • Talent marketplace matching?
  • Learning recommendations?
  • Succession planning?
  • Workforce planning?
  • Recruiting and hiring?
  • Regulatory or compliance requirements?

3. If you use a 5-point proficiency scale, what measurable value does it provide?

We are particularly curious about:

  • Better internal mobility decisions
  • More accurate Opportunity Marketplace recommendations
  • Improved Career Development Planning (CDP)
  • Better Career & Talent Development (CTD) experiences
  • Skill gap analysis
  • Workforce planning
  • Learning recommendations
  • Succession planning

Have you been able to demonstrate business outcomes that justify maintaining proficiency ratings?

4. Have you experienced challenges with proficiency scales?

For example:

  • Employees overrating themselves
  • Lack of assessment consistency
  • Manager calibration effort
  • Skills becoming outdated
  • Low employee engagement in updating ratings
  • Difficulty explaining the difference between levels 3, 4, and 5

Would you make the same design decision again?

5. Has anyone simplified from a 5-point scale to a binary model (Have / Don't Have)?

If yes:

  • What was the driver?
  • What was gained?
  • What was lost?
  • Did adoption improve?
  • Did reporting become easier?
  • Did you see any negative impact on Opportunity Marketplace, CDP, CTD, Learning, or Talent processes?

6. Do you use different proficiency models for different skill categories?

Examples:

Skill Type Scale

CertificationsCertified / Not Certified
Compliance SkillsQualified / Not Qualified
Technical Skills5-point scale
Language SkillsCEFR scale
Soft Skills3-point or binary

Has anyone found success with a mixed approach rather than one global proficiency model?

7. How important is proficiency to the following SuccessFactors capabilities?

We would love to hear practical examples regarding:

  • Opportunity Marketplace
  • Talent Intelligence Hub
  • Career Development Planning (CDP)
  • Career & Talent Development (CTD)
  • Learning recommendations
  • Performance & Goals
  • Succession Management
  • Recruiting
  • Position Management
  • SmartRecruiters integration
  • Onboarding
  • Crossboarding
  • Workforce Planning

For each area, did proficiency significantly improve outcomes, or was skill existence itself sufficient?

The Fundamental Question

If you were designing your skills architecture again today:

Would you implement a detailed 5-point proficiency scale, or would you choose a simpler approach such as Have/Have-Not and focus more on skill validation, certifications, experiences, and skill discovery?

We are particularly interested in hearing:

  • Lessons learned
  • Adoption data
  • Manager feedback
  • Employee feedback
  • Business outcomes
  • Things you would do differently

Thank you in advance for sharing your experiences.

entry.title: Provisioning a Solution in SAP Data Accelerator: A Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
Hi, community!

SAP is introducing SAP Data Accelerator, an SAP-managed service that enables secure access to data from supported SAP systems through authorized partner solutions. It replaces the customer-managed connectivity setup used in supported partner access scenarios with an SAP-operated service layer designed to support enterprise security, regulatory compliance, and operational availability, while helping reduce brittle integrations, simplify operations, and minimize transition effort.

This guide walks you through the end-to-end process of provisioning a new solution in SAP Data Accelerator using SAP for Me. It is intended for administrators and technical users who need to set up and access a new SAP Data Accelerator tenant. By the end of this guide, you will have a fully provisioned solution and the credentials to sign in to it.

Step 1: Open SAP for Me

Open SAP for Me in your browser and navigate to Portfolio & Products > Applications and choose Start Provisioning in the SAP Data Accelerator Hub tile. 

SAP for Me dashboard showing Applications in Products & Portfolio

Step 2: Use the Create Solution wizard

The Create Solution wizard opens and displays four steps: General Information, Select Applications, Configure Parameters, and Review. The wizard starts at Step 1: General Information. Wait for any loading indicator to finish before proceeding.

Step 3: Enter General Information

Fill in the required information: Name (1), Path (2), and choose Next Step (3).

General Information page

Step 4: Enter Configure Parameters

Fill in the required information: Region, Cloud Connector Auth Data Password, External System Name, Certificate (PEM): copy and paste the entire certificate from "begin" to "end", External System Name, Certificate (PEM): copy and paste the entire certificate from "begin" to "end", and choose Review.

Step 5: Review the Information

Review the information entered and if everything looks correct, choose Finish.

Step 6: Download the Authentication Data

You will receive a pop-up screen informing you that provisioning has started and that the resources are being provisioned in the selected resource group. Choose the View in Resources button to check the status of your request. While the tenant is in Processing state (1), a View Notifications button (2) becomes available. Use this button to access the Download connection details link (3).

Tenant Notifications screen

Step 7: Connect the Cloud Connector to SAP Data Accelerator 

The authentication data downloaded from your SAP Data Accelerator instance includes the subaccount details required for the setup in Palantir's platform, such as the region, subaccount, login email address, and password.

Subaccount details

Use the information from those details to connect the Cloud Connector to the SAP Data Accelerator subaccount, as described in the SAP Help Portal page Connecting to SAP Data Accelerator. Complete this step within 5 minutes of downloading the authentication data. The activation link expires 1 hour after it is generated.

Step 8: Start Using SAP Data Accelerator 

Once the account has been activated, your SAP Data Accelerator is ready to use (1). You can log in to your SAP Data Accelerator either using your SAP Data Accelerator URL or using the Main URL provided in the Access Information (2) of the SAP Data Accelerator tenant.

Account ready to use

The purpose of this blog post is to guide you step by step through the process of provisioning a solution in SAP Data Accelerator. For a list of the prerequisites and more information, I suggest you visit the SAP Help Portal documentation Provisioning SAP Data Accelerator.

If you liked reading this post, feel free to share it with your colleagues. Also, I invite you to follow my profile and leave a comment or a question. 

All the best,

Eduardo

entry.title: Re: Connecting SAP Sales Cloud V2 to a Standalone SAP Analytics Cloud - A Practical Alternative
entry.content:
Thanks for testing it and reporting back @s_schneemann1.

Quick clarification on the namespace error: the reference build is OData V4, and the metadata document always declares an explicit Namespace (it's a fixed constant, not derived at runtime), so a missing namespace isn't something the generator itself would produce. That points to the gap being somewhere in the delivery path rather than the protocol choice: something between your build and SAC receiving the $metadata response as clean, valid XML (auth handling, a proxy layer, or content-type on the response).

Your OData V2 route is a solid call regardless, it skips the custom metadata generation entirely, so there's less surface for that specific class of issue.

If you want to dig into where the V4 metadata call broke down, happy to help think it through, might be useful to document for others hitting the same wall.

entry.title: Re: ERROR IN ACCOUNT DETERMINATION ID
entry.content:
Hi:

It is actually more than that. You need to reverse the bills and then reverse the movein.  Then you can create a new movein with the right ADID and rebill it.  The ADID is not changeable at the contract level. 

Or you could do a movein/out as of the current date to get the right data moving forward.  And decide if you want to post manual journal entry to the correct revenue accounts based on the desired ADID.  In that case there is not much customer impact.

regards,

bill.

entry.title: Re: Import button not showing in the display dimension APP
entry.content:
Hi,

The docuentation that SAP has for SAP LGM will be updated soon, but to your situation, you will no more have an import Button to manually import your UoMs and Dims, it's all now part of the data replication procedure, 
So, first make sure you have imported your org sturcture so you have your Logical system set up, then go ahead and set up the manage system connection in sap LGM  as mentioned here ( https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_LOGISTICS_MANAGEMENT/ff6700aed66c43c48fe5351d55db2381/e866992a033e4132b6bc9e4074bb63cb.html?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=SHIP), you will mainly need to set up connection 3001, make sure to check : SAP Customizing Implementation Guide  Logistics - General  Material Master  Basic Settings  Define Output Format of Material Numbers. when setting up. Y ou will also need your CPI and basis team support incase the connection check fails.

Second : once the system connection 3001 is set upm you will use the Manage replication configuration app to create a replication for Business object type : SAP S/4HANA Customizing Data, then you will use the schedule Data Replication app to run the job for the SAP S/4HANA Customizing Data object type, you can set it to run immediately. 

Finally you can validate your UoMs and Dims, as well as code lists ...



Hope this helps

entry.title: Re: SAP Datasphere: Data Tiering Strategy and Best Practices
entry.content:
Hi @jeet_kapase 

Thank you for the article. I have one question regarding the practical implementation of Pattern 3.

The article explains the concept of promoting data from the Object Store (HDLF) to the Warm and Hot tiers, but I couldn't find how this is technically implemented in SAP Datasphere. How do you typically move data between Object Store spaces and SAP HANA Database spaces? Are there any recommended mechanisms or best practices?

I'm asking because customer-managed Data Products that are shared via SAP Business Data Cloud Connect (e.g. with Snowflake) are stored in the Object Store. At the same time, many development teams primarily work with SQL, while Transformation Flows in Object Store spaces currently support only Python logic.

Since Replication Flows currently do not support SAP Datasphere-to-SAP Datasphere replication (including between Object Store and SAP HANA Database spaces), I'm wondering what the recommended approach is for implementing these data movements and transformation pipelines.

Do you have any recommendations or best practices for organizing transformation pipelines and data movement between these two space types?

entry.title: Re: How can a sub-WBS element be created in SAP S/4HANA P...
entry.content:
Hi Dnyaneshwari,

Short answer: Create the sub-WBS as a lower-level WBS element in the Project Control – Enterprise Projects app. The workaround works because the "one demand per activity type" rule is evaluated per WBS element. The demand assignment itself is FI/CO-neutral (a demand is not a cost object), but in an investment project the new sub-WBS is a real cost collector that gets its own Asset under Construction (AuC) and its own settlement path.

How is a sub-WBS element created, and why does that resolve the conflict?

Create it in the Project Control – Enterprise Projects app by adding a lower-level WBS element under the parent in the project structure (sub app - Project Planning). Enterprise projects support multi-level WBS hierarchies; there are no networks/activities in Public Cloud, only WBS elements.

Why it works: For a human-resource (team resource) demand, the activity type is a key attribute and only one demand with the same activity type is allowed per WBS element. A sub-WBS is a separate WBS element, so a demand with the same Cost Center + Activity Type can be created on it. See also this post: SAP Community

Constraints to verify:

  • Verify: Confirm you actually need a second demand. One HR demand can have multiple people staffed on it (one or more per demand). If you only need more effort/persons under the same activity type, a second demand — and therefore a sub-WBS — may be unnecessary. Check the post above.

Does assigning a demand to a sub-WBS affect FI/CO in an investment project?

No — for the demand itself. Yes, with limits — for the sub-WBS you create.

A demand is not a separate cost object or a separate structure element; it is only an assignment to a project or WBS element. Assigning it therefore triggers no posting on its own.
Reference: SAP Help Portal

Constraint: The sub-WBS is an account-assignment/cost object. In an investment project the system automatically creates a separate AuC for each account-assigned WBS element, and each WBS must be settled to its own AuC and then to the final fixed asset. So a new sub-WBS = a new AuC + a new settlement path.
Reference: SAP Community

How does the demand function on the sub-WBS, and how do main vs. sub-WBS relate (cost collection, reporting, settlement)?

Demand function (labour): Staffing people on the HR demand prefills their timesheets with the planned effort; the demand is a planning placeholder and creates no commitment. SAP Community

Cost collection: Actual labour cost posts when time is recorded/approved (activity allocation via Cost Center + Activity Type rate) onto the WBS element the demand belongs to — i.e. the sub-WBS, not the parent. All such execution transactions lead to actual cost postings on the WBS element they are assigned to, and the account assignment object for actual postings in an investment project is always the WBS element on which costs are collected.
References:  SAP Community and SAP Community

Reporting: Each WBS element is a discrete cost collector; sub-WBS costs are visible individually and roll up the WBS hierarchy to the parent and project header in standard project reporting (Project Cost Report, Project Profitability). (Deduction — standard WBS hierarchy aggregation; not a demand-specific behavior.)

Settlement: In an investment project each account-assigned WBS settles its own collected costs to its own AuC, then on to the final fixed asset — the sub-WBS settles independently of the parent.

Implications specific to labour-only demand planning

Important:

  • Capitalization: Labour recorded on an investment-project WBS is collected there and settled to AuC (i.e. capitalized); costs that must not be capitalized have to be routed to a cost center/GL via the settlement setup. Decide upfront whether the labour on the sub-WBS should be capitalized before letting it settle. Reference: SAP Community
  • Availability: Staffing an HR demand does not reduce workforce availability unless you use SAP Project and Resource Management / external staffing. With external staffing activated (config 106941, irreversible), resource assignments must be maintained in the external tool and only displayed in Manage Project Demand. Reference: KBA 3660165  SAP CommunitySAP
  • Structure hygiene: Every extra sub-WBS multiplies AuCs and settlement objects at period close — keep the structure as flat as the activity-type requirement allows.

Summary: Add the sub-WBS in Project Control – Enterprise Projects to host the second same-activity-type labour demand. The demand assignment is FI/CO-neutral, but the sub-WBS is a genuine cost object with its own AuC and settlement in an investment project. Before doing it, confirm you can't simply staff more people on the existing demand, and decide whether that labour should be capitalized (settle to AuC) or moved to a cost center/GL.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Where to find the change log for recurring JE template
entry.content:
Dear @AIDream,

The Log Number in Manage Recurring Journal Entries should not be used to check changes made to the recurring JE template. This app is used to manage recurring journal entries and review posting status, while the recurring posting job generates the actual accounting journal entries from the recurring original entries. To check who changed the recurring entry and what was changed use Display Changes of Recurring Entry / FBD4. In this app, select Recurring Entry Documents and enter the relevant company code, recurring document number and fiscal year. The change output shows the changed user, date/time, field name, old value and new value

 

 

For already-posted journal entries created from the recurring entry, the change log should be checked separately in journal entry apps, for example via Manage Journal Entries, which supports analysis of change and reversal logs

More details below:

SAP Help – Manage Recurring Journal Entries
SAP Help – Post Recurring Journal Entries
SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library – Display Changes of Recurring Entry / FBD4
SAP Help – Manage Journal Entries

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: 🐍 July Developer Challenge — Snake & Ladder with SAP CAP Status Transition Flows
entry.content:
SAP Developer Challenge - Snake & Ladder with SAP CAP Status Transition Flows

Welcome to the July 2026 SAP Developer Challenge — a 4-week hands-on series where you build a multiplayer Snake & Ladder game as a fully functional SAP CAP service, powered by CAP Status Flows (@flow.status).

The Scenario

We will be building the backend engine for an online Snake & Ladder game. Each player's position on the board is a state — and every move, snake bite, and ladder climb is a status transition modelled using CAP's @flow.status annotations.

By the end of July, you'll have:

  • A running CAP Node.js service with CDS entities and status-flow bound actions
  • A multiplayer game engine with turn management, dice rolls, snake/ladder triggers, and CAP events
  • A SAP Fiori UI displaying the live board — participants just plug in their service URL
  • Integration with the CAP MCP server to let an AI assistant drive moves via natural language

What Makes This Interesting

CAP's @flow.status feature gives you out-of-the-box:

  • Pre-validation — a @from: [#Playing] annotation rejects any action called in the wrong state (HTTP 409), no custom code needed
  • Automatic state transitions — @TO: #Finished updates the entity after your action returns
  • History tracking — @TO: $flow.previous restores the previous state (perfect for a snake bite that temporarily blocks a player)
  • Fiori integration — buttons in the UI are automatically enabled/disabled based on the current status

Snake & Ladder maps directly onto this model: a player's turn is a state machine, and every square is a potential transition trigger.

Challenge Format

Each week a blog post is published on SAP Community with the task, hints, and code guidance. Submit your answer as a reply comment on that week's blog post.

Week Dates Title Core Topic Blog Post

11–7 JulModel the BoardCDS entities, status enum, @flow.statuslink
28–14 JulRoll & MoveBound actions, @from / @to transitionsTBD
315–21 JulMultiplayer & EventsTurn management, CAP events, snake/ladder logicTBD
422–31 JulFiori UI & CAP MCPBoard UI, MCP serverTBD

Prerequisites

  • Node.js 20+
  • @sap/cds-dk installed globally: npm install -g @sap/cds-dk
  • VS Code with the SAP CDS Language Support extension
  • Basic familiarity with CAP (entities, services, cds watch)

No prior knowledge of CAP status flows is required — Week 1 introduces everything from scratch.

The Board

The game uses a classic 10×10 board (squares 1–100). Key squares:

Ladders (climb up)

Bottom Top Effect

238Big early boost
831Good start
1526Small step up
2142Mid-board jump
2884The long ladder — almost wins it
3644Short gain
5167Solid advance
7191Late-game boost
7898So close to victory

Single-Headed Snakes (slide back)

Head Tail Severity

997Devastating — one square from the finish
7620Major setback
4318Mid-game punisher

Double-Headed Snakes (two biting ends, one shared tail)

These special snakes have two heads on different squares that share a single tail. Landing on either head square sends you to the same tail square.

Head 1 Head 2 Shared Tail

897250
546334
40473

The double-headed snake maps to CAP's @from: [#Head1, #Head2] — the same transition triggered by multiple entry states.

Reusable Board UI

A ready-made HTML/JS board will be provided later. Participants plug in their CAP service URL and see the live board — no frontend coding required for the challenge.

Resources

    • CAP Status Transition Flows Documentation
    • CAP Node.js Documentation
    • Initial Setup

entry.title: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
Week 1 — Model the Board

Challenge series: Snake & Ladder Developer Challenge  | Week 1 of 4 Dates: 1–7 July 2026

The Setup

Every game needs a board. Before any snake can bite or ladder can lift, you need a data model that captures the state of a game — players, positions, and crucially, whose turn it is and what can happen next.

This week we will build the CDS foundation: entities, a status enum, and your first @flow.status annotation. No game logic yet — just clean modelling and a running CAP service.

What You'll Build This Week

  • A new CAP Node.js project: snake-ladder
  • CDS entities for GameSession, Player, and BoardSquare
  • A TurnStatus enum with the game states
  • Your first @flow.status annotation
  • Seed data: a 10×10 board with all snakes and ladders pre-loaded
  • A running service at http://localhost:4004

Prerequisites

  • Node.js 20+
  • @SAP/cds-dk installed globally:
npm install -g @sap/cds-dk
  • VS Code with the SAP CDS Language Support extension. Check Initial Setup for more details how to do a local setup. 

Background: CAP Status Flows in 60 Seconds

CAP's @flow.status feature turns a regular enum field into a controlled state machine. You annotate:

  • The entity with @flow.status: FieldName to designate which field drives the state machine
  • Bound actions with @from: [#StateA] to declare which states allow that action
  • Bound actions with @TO: #StateB to declare the target state after the action runs

CAP handles the rest automatically:

  • Validates the current state before the action runs (returns HTTP 409 if wrong state)
  • Updates the status field after the action completes
  • Generates Fiori annotations to enable/disable buttons based on current state

No custom validation code. No manual status updates. The framework enforces the rules.

Step-by-Step

1. Initialise the CAP project

cds init snake-ladder --nodejs
cd snake-ladder
npm install
code .

2. Create the data model

Create db/schema.cds:

namespace snakeladder;

// ── Status enum — the heart of the state machine ──────────────────────────
type TurnStatus : String enum {
  Waiting;    // Session created, not yet started
  Playing;    // It's this player's turn — roll the dice!
  Moving;     // Dice rolled, player token is being moved
  Blocked;    // Player hit a double-headed snake — skips next turn
  Finished;   // This player has reached square 100 and won
}

// ── Board square — pre-loaded with snake/ladder data ──────────────────────
entity BoardSquares {
  key square      : Integer;        // 1–100
      snakeTo     : Integer;        // 0 if no snake on this square
      ladderTo    : Integer;        // 0 if no ladder on this square
      isDoubleHead: Boolean default false;  // true if this is a double-headed snake mouth
}

// ── Game session ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
entity GameSessions {
  key ID          : UUID;
      name        : String(100);    // e.g. "Friday Afternoon Game"
      createdAt   : Timestamp;
      startedAt   : Timestamp;
      finishedAt  : Timestamp;
      winner      : Association to Players;
}

// ── Player — one row per player per session ───────────────────────────────
@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
  key ID          : UUID;
      session     : Association to GameSessions;
      name        : String(100);
      @readonly
      position    : Integer default 1;      // current square (1–100)
      @readonly
      TurnStatus  : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
      turnOrder   : Integer;                // 1 = goes first
      lastRoll    : Integer;               // last dice result
}

Why @flow.status on Players? Each player is an independent state machine. Their TurnStatus transitions independently — one player is Playing while others are Waiting.

3. Create the service

Create srv/game-service.cds:

using { snakeladder } from '../db/schema';

service GameService {

  entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

  @flow.status: TurnStatus
  entity Players as projection on snakeladder.Players actions {

    // Week 2 will implement these — declare them now so the model is complete
    @from: [ #Waiting ]
    @to: #Playing
    action startTurn();

    @from: [ #Playing ]
    @to: #Moving
    action rollDice() returns Integer;

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @to: #Waiting                  // transitions to next player in Week 3
    action confirmMove();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @to: #Blocked                  // double-headed snake bite
    action blockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Blocked ]
    @to: $flow.previous            // restores pre-block status
    action unblockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @to: #Finished
    action winGame();
  }

  entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

4. Add seed data

Create db/data/snakeladder-GameSessions.csv:

ID,name,createdAt,startedAt,finishedAt,winner_ID
a1b2c3d4-0001-0000-0000-000000000000,Demo Game,2026-07-01T09:00:00Z,,

Create db/data/snakeladder-BoardSquares.csv:

square,snakeTo,ladderTo,isDoubleHead
1,0,0,false
2,0,38,false

4,0,0,false

Double-headed snake logic: Squares 40, 47, 54, 63, 72, and 89 all have isDoubleHead: true. Pairs (40,47), (54,63), and (72,89) share a tail. Your Week 2 move handler will use this flag to apply the correct snakeTo destination.

5. Run and verify

cds watch

Open http://localhost:4004. You should see GameService with endpoints for GameSessions, Players, and BoardSquares.

Test the board data is loaded:

GET http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/GameService/BoardSquares?$filter=snakeTo gt 0

You should see all 9 snake-head squares returned.

How to Submit

Reply to the Week 1 blog post on SAP Community with a comment containing:

  1. Your db/schema.cds — paste as a CDS code block
  2. Your srv/game-service.cds — paste as a CDS code block
  3. A screenshot of http://localhost:4004 showing the GameService endpoints
  4. One sentence explaining your choice of TurnStatus enum values — did you add any of your own?

What's Next

In Week 2 — Roll & Move, you implement the bound actions. You'll write the rollDice handler, apply snake and ladder logic, and see CAP automatically enforce the @from / @TO transitions — returning HTTP 409 when a player tries to roll out of turn.

Resources

    • CAP Status Flows Documentation
    • CAP Node.js Documentation
    • Initial Setup 
    • cds-cli : cds add http

entry.title: Re: AI Powered Assistance in SAP Learning Hub
entry.content:
@Arulgsenthil 

Good question, this is SAP's new integration of Google's NotebookLM into SAP Learning Hub, and it's a nice addition for exam prep. 

When you click "Access AI Learning" (shown as the tile "Upskill faster with AI-powered assistance") on a course, it opens a new browser tab with a NotebookLM workspace that's pre-filled with the course content you selected, and you only need to set up your workspace once. 

  • Chat with the course content and ask questions in your language of choice, getting precise, cited answers grounded in SAP's trusted learning content
  • Listen to an AI-generated podcast/audio overview of the material
  • Use interactive study guides, mind maps, FAQs, and summaries to reinforce learning

This mirrors the general capability described in SAP and Google Cloud's announcement, where a "Upskill faster with AI" option takes learners into a NotebookLM environment pre-populated with relevant learning curated by SAP and integrated via the NotebookLM API, with the environment kept read-only so content quality assurance stays with SAP.

A couple of important nuances:

  • Your current notebook license grants viewer rights only, so you can explore and interact with the pre-generated notebooks, but you can't create your own notebooks or upload your own sources into this environment.
  • This feature isn't currently included in the SAP Learning Hub event edition, student edition, or the edition for SAP Enterprise Support, so availability depends on which edition your subscription is on.
  • Notebooks are available for most standalone courses on learning.sap.com, but not for BTP trial courses, translated courses, or courses originally offered on openSAP.
  • For access issues, make sure you're opening the notebook through the SAP Learning site itself rather than a direct link shared by someone else.

On cost: You're right, there's no separate or extra charge for using this. It's included as part of an active SAP Learning Hub subscription (on the eligible editions above), so if you already have access to the learning journey, the AI assistant tile comes with it.

Hope that helps clarify things. Happy studying!

entry.title: SAP MBC SFTP Onboarding: Common Setup Mistakes and Quick Fixes
entry.content:
1. Missing Test File Name Can Cause Unexpected Errors

While performing “Test Connection” in SFTP Gate, the connection may appear correct, but the test still fails with unclear errors.

One common reason is:

  • Test File Name field is left empty

This results in failure during the upload test, even though connectivity and authentication are working correctly.

Fix: Always provide a simple test file name, for example:

test.txt

2. Extra Spaces in Directory Path Can Lead to 502 Errors

A very small formatting issue can cause misleading errors:

  • Leading or trailing spaces in directory path

Example:

Incorrect : " /mahi/bng/ReverseFile/"
Correct   : "/mahi/bng/ReverseFile/"

This may result in:

  • 502 Internal Server Error
  • Directory access failures

Fix:

  • Clear the field completely
  • Re-type the directory manually
  • Avoid copy-paste

3. Connection “PASS” Does Not Always Mean Fully Configured

Even when:

  • SFTP connection succeeds
  • Credentials are valid

The overall test may still fail due to:

  • Missing directories
  • Incorrect file paths
  • Incomplete configuration

Recommendation: Validate:

  • Outbound directory (payments)
  • Inbound directory (responses)
  • Test file upload

4. Use Simple File Mask for Initial Testing

When configuring inbound processing:

If a strict file mask is used too early, it may prevent files from being picked up.

 Suggested approach:

Start with: .*
Refine later once file naming is confirmed

5. Always Verify Directory Availability with the Bank

If errors persist after correcting configuration, the issue may be on the bank side.

Common causes include:

  • Directory does not exist
  • Incorrect path (case-sensitive)
  • Missing read/write permissions

 It is helpful to confirm with the bank:

  • Folder exists
  • User has appropriate access
  • Correct directory path is being used

Conclusion

These issues may look small, but they can take time to identify during onboarding.

While I am still learning in this area, following a structured approach helped me troubleshoot faster and avoid unnecessary escalations.

I hope these observations help others working on SAP MBC SFTP integrations.

References

For more detailed information, you may refer to SAP Help documentation:

  • SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity (SFTP H2H) Documentation
    https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

entry.title: Re: I have a question about accounting entries for credit risk-based impairment provisioning.
entry.content:
SAP Machine Translation Portal translation:

I need to know if I can write a credit-risk-based impairment accounting entry to debit "credit impairment loss", credit as "reserve for bad debt", and do not want to post directly to a reconciliation account

entry.title: Re: I have a question about accounting entries for credit...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Credit-Risk-Based Impairment Accounting Entry

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, when posting a credit-risk-based impairment, you can indeed debit ‘Credit Impairment Loss’ and credit ‘Reserve for Bad Debt’. This process does not require direct posting to a reconciliation account.

Key Points:

  • Account Determination: You only need to set up profit and loss (P&L) accounts for the impairment postings. The postings are made directly to the account to be valuated,  which means you can avoid using reconciliation accounts. For more information, see Configuration Requirements Checklist for Credit-Risk Based-Impairment.
  • Posting Process: The impairment job calculates a loss allowance for expected credit losses  on trade receivables and G/L account balances. It posts the results as impairment losses or creates posting proposals for impairment for the creation of your financial statements. For more information, see Post Credit-Risk-Based Impairment.

Example:

  • Debit: Credit Impairment Loss
  • Credit: Reserve for Bad Debt

This setup allows for flexibility in managing your financial statements without the need for reconciliation accounts.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Understanding 502 Errors in SAP MBC SFTP Connections (Practical Troubleshooting Guide)
entry.content:
What Does a 502 Error Mean in This Context?

In SAP MBC SFTP testing, a 502 Internal Server Error does not necessarily indicate a system outage or backend failure.

In many cases, it can be triggered due to:

  • Incorrect configuration values
  • Invalid directory paths
  • Missing or incomplete test inputs
  • Access or permission issues on the bank side

Understanding this helps avoid unnecessary escalation and speeds up troubleshooting.

Common Causes of 502 Errors

Based on practical experience, the following are some common reasons behind 502 errors:

1. Missing Test File Name

During “Test Connection,” the upload step requires a file name.

If the field is left empty:

  • Upload operation fails
  • Error may appear as 502

✅ Fix:
Provide a simple file name:

test.txt

2. Incorrect Directory Path (Formatting Issues)

Even a small formatting issue can lead to failure.

Example:

Incorrect:  " /mahi/bng/ReverseFile/"
Correct:    "/mahi/bng/ReverseFile/"

Issues may include:

  • Leading spaces
  • Trailing spaces
  • Incorrect casing

Fix:

  • Clear the field completely
  • Re-type the path manually
  • Avoid copy-paste

3. Directory Does Not Exist

Sometimes the configuration is correct, but the directory:

  • Is not yet created on the bank SFTP server
  • Has a slightly different name
  • Is case-sensitive and mismatched

Fix:
Confirm with the bank:

  • Directory exists
  • Path is correct
  • Naming is exactly matching

4. Insufficient Permissions

Even if the directory exists, the user may not have:

  • Read access (for inbound)
  • Write access (for outbound)

This can result in failures during listing or upload.

Fix:
Ask the bank to verify:

  • User permissions
  • Ability to list/read/write files

5. Temporary Backend/UI Timeouts

In some cases, especially during activation or first-time testing:

  • The system may take time to respond
  • UI may timeout and show 502

Fix:

  • Refresh the screen
  • Retry after a few seconds
  • Validate again before making changes

Step-by-Step Troubleshooting Approach

A simple structured approach helped avoid confusion:

Step 1 → Validate basic configuration (host, port, credentials)

Step 2 → Check Test File Name is provided

Step 3 → Verify directory path (no spaces, correct format)

Step 4 → Test with base directory (if needed, e.g. /bapuna/)

Step 5 → Confirm with bank (directory existence and permissions)

Key Takeaways

  • 502 errors are often configuration-related, not system failures
  • Small issues like spaces or missing inputs can cause major confusion
  • Always validate basic inputs first before assuming backend issues
  • Coordinating with the bank early helps reduce delays

Conclusion

While working through these scenarios, I realized that troubleshooting becomes much easier with a structured approach and attention to small details.

I am still building my understanding in this area, but these learnings helped me handle similar issues more confidently.

I hope this helps others facing similar challenges in SAP MBC SFTP onboarding.

References

For more details, you may refer to SAP Help documentation:

  • SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity (SFTP H2H) Documentation
    https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - Operations - User, Personas and Role Collection
entry.content:
Dear Community,

this blog provides practical insights into a proper User Management for SAP Integration Suite.

Intention

SAP Integration Suite uses Identity and Access Management to authenticate users through an identity provider (by default, SAP ID Service) and manage their authorizations. Permissions are assigned via predefined groups and roles such as AuthGroup.IntegrationDeveloper, which include roles for tasks like designing and deploying integration flows. By default, these personas already cover most of the requirements. However, a closer look could provide a more dedicated understanding on this topic.

Approach

Each capability in SAP Integration Suite comes with its own role collections, users must be assigned based on the persona that matches their responsibilities.
According to our landscape, we also want to consider different authorizations in the different stages. As a simple example, in each stage we need an administrator, but there should be no developer in Production.
In our former SAP middleware system, this was easily controlled by setting the system to “not editable.” However, this is now something we can only achieve through user roles. 

Solution

Capability - Cloud Integration (simpel)

Stage: DEV

PersonaRole Collection
Administratorby default PI_Administrator or custom (next table)
Developer

by default PI_Integration_Developer or custom (next table) 

During SAP PO Migration journey you can think of this additional Role Collections: PIMAS_Administrator, PIMAS_IntegrationAnalyst

Transportercustom: WorkspacePackagesTransport

Stage TEST /QAS

PersonaRole Collection
Administratorby default PI_Administrator or custom (next table)
Business Experteby default PI_Business_Expert  or custom (next table)
Monitoringcustom: See table below
Monitoring_sensitivecustom: See table below
Operatorcustom: See table below
Operator_sensitivecustom: See table below
EditorPI_Integration_Developer (to be assigned with Work Package edit rights, similar to a firefighter role, allowing temporary editing in Production for limited and justified purposes).
Auditor/ViewerAuthGroup_ReadOnly

Stage PROD

PersonaRole Collection
Administratorby default PI_Administrator or custom (next table)
Business Experteby default PI_Business_Expert  or custom (See custom table below)
Monitoringcustom: See table below
Monitoring_sensitivecustom: See table below
Operatorcustom: See table below
Operator_sensitivecustom: See table below
EditorPI_Integration_Developer (to be assigned with Work Package edit rights, similar to a firefighter role, allowing temporary editing in Production for limited and justified purposes).
Auditor/ViewerAuthGroup_ReadOnly

independent if productive enterprise landscapes or a Environments with high compliance/audit pressure

More capabilities coming soon: API Management, EIC, and Event Mesh.

Conclusion: 

A well-defined user and role concept is a key success factor for operating SAP Integration Suite securely and efficiently across all landscape stages. While SAP’s standard role collections already cover most common use cases, custom role collections allow organizations to implement a clearer separation of duties, comply with audit requirements, and follow the principle of least privilege.
